author: Early Corpus Analyst Cedar

dimensionSummaries.dimension.player-fantasy: The player is a temporal problem-solver who restores a damaged land by learning how actions, structures, and relationships differ between past and present.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.core-loop: The cadence moves between information gathering, past-present comparison, puzzle-dense field and dungeon routes, new tools or harp tunes, and essence recovery.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.verbs: Sword and shield actions combine with lifting, jumping, swimming, seed effects, ranged tools, object exchange, and increasingly flexible time travel through the Harp of Ages.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.world-state: Past and present maintain related but different terrain, construction, and NPC states; permanent tools, tunes, essences, trades, rings, and linked secrets accumulate.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.topology: A compact screen-grid region exists in two temporal versions whose corresponding locations may differ in water, buildings, routes, settlements, and interaction opportunities.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.gating-revisit: Harp tunes, time portals, movement equipment, dungeon tools, and knowledge of cross-era causality reopen places whose useful state exists in another time.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.puzzle-information: Clues and visible differences invite players to form hypotheses about temporal cause, object state, switches, color, movement, and multi-room dependencies.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.dungeon-structure: Eight principal dungeons use keyed room graphs, signature tools, multi-room state puzzles, bosses, and essence rewards, with later challenges increasing dependency density.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.exploration-reward: Temporal detours, caves, seed trees, rings, trades, companion paths, and linked secrets provide upgrades, resources, shortcuts, and new causal information.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.growth-economy: Hearts, rupees, bombs, seeds, rings, upgrades, and collected materials support preparedness while new harp functions expand access without consuming a resource.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.combat-boss: Combat remains top-down and tool-aware, but many bounded challenges place execution inside puzzle-like state constraints before bosses test observation and control.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.npc-quest: Residents occupy era-specific situations, give clues and exchanges, and can respond differently as actions or information connect the two times and paired game.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.narrative-performance: The possessed oracle and disrupted history make temporal damage visible; rescues, era shifts, essence beats, and linked continuation pace the restoration narrative.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.camera-blocking: Fixed screens preserve landmarks for past-present comparison, while a time-shift effect and changed actor or terrain blocking stage the consequence in the same location.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.teaching-feedback: Early portal use shows one coordinate in two eras, and each additional harp tune expands transition freedom with a visible effect and nearby route application.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.ui-input: Two assignable item buttons and a subscreen manage weapons, tools, seeds, rings, and the harp; stable counters keep health and expendables legible during puzzle action.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.recovery-accessibility: Persistent saves retain tools, tunes, essences, and key quest state, while healing and compact routes support retries; temporal puzzles must remain solvable from either recoverable state.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.perspective-implementation: The 2D top-down view makes corresponding coordinates easy to compare, with some side-view passages adding gravity-based traversal under the same inventory system.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.hardware-multiplayer: The original Game Boy Color title is single-player but shares state asynchronously with its paired adventure through passwords and limited link functions.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.production-runtime: Its model requires paired temporal maps, cross-era state dependencies, legal transition anchors and tunes, persistent items, item assignment, linked secrets, and temporal anti-softlock tests.

observationThemes.context: At a coordinate where the current harp function permits transition, the player can use the Harp of Ages to move between the region's past and present.
preconditions: The harp and an applicable tune have been acquired
The current location satisfies that tune's transition rule
before: Only the current era's terrain and actors are active
A route or causal opportunity may be unavailable
after: The corresponding location in the other era becomes active
Changed terrain, structures, or characters can be traversed or influenced
feedback: Music, transition effects, palette, scenery, and NPC differences mark the era change
The harp's expanded tune set remains visible in inventory progression
consequences: Temporal comparison becomes part of the repeated play loop
New tunes increase where and how the player can perform the world-changing verb
observationThemes.factualDescription: The Harp of Ages and its learned tunes let the player transition between past and present under progressively broader location rules, activating the corresponding temporal version of the area.
observationThemes.interpretation: Treating time travel as an acquired, repeatedly invoked verb makes temporal authorship part of player identity rather than reserving it for scripted scenes.

observationThemes.context: A location has related past and present versions, and an object, structure, or route condition can be reached or altered more effectively in one era.
preconditions: Both temporal states of the region are reachable
The player has observed a blocked route or state difference
playerInput: Change or traverse the era-specific condition
Return to the corresponding location if needed
before: The target route is blocked in the current time
The paired coordinate suggests a different causal state
action: Use temporal transition and perform the available era-specific action
after: The route state or reachable position differs
A destination, shortcut, or reward can be accessed
feedback: Stable landmark relationships identify the coordinate
Changed buildings, terrain, water, or inhabitants show the temporal consequence
consequences: The player gains progression or optional value
Earlier places become multi-era causal questions
observationThemes.factualDescription: Corresponding locations in past and present can differ in traversability and object or settlement state, so acting or routing through one era can make a destination reachable.
observationThemes.interpretation: Temporal navigation is legible when geography stays comparable but selected causal features differ, allowing players to reason from contrast rather than search two unrelated maps.

observationThemes.context: A dungeon dependency spans several rooms or floors, with switches, object positions, movement tools, or color states influencing a route beyond the immediate screen.
preconditions: The relevant dungeon tool or rule has been introduced
playerInput: Navigate to the affected room and test the result
before: The remote door, platform, or path is in the wrong state
The dungeon map contains an unresolved dependency
action: Manipulate the shared state and traverse the resulting room sequence
after: The player reaches a key, boss path, or objective
feedback: Color, sound, object orientation, door animation, or changed geometry identifies the state
Repeated symbols link cause and distant effect
consequences: The dungeon graph advances toward its boss and essence
The player learns to preserve and reason about state across room boundaries
observationThemes.factualDescription: Principal dungeons include dependencies in which changing a visible switch, object, or movement state affects routes across more than one room before the boss and essence objective.
observationThemes.interpretation: A bounded challenge can increase puzzle depth by asking the player to carry a clearly signaled state through space, provided feedback makes the remote relationship reconstructible.

observationThemes.context: A resident or settlement occupies a different situation in past and present, and conversation or an item exchange in the relevant era advances a local need.
preconditions: The required era and resident are reachable
The player possesses the relevant item, information, or prior quest state
playerInput: Approach and speak with the resident
Provide the required help or information
before: The character's need or settlement condition remains unresolved
A related route, clue, or reward is unavailable
after: Dialogue and persistent quest state update
A clue, item, or changed opportunity becomes available
feedback: Fixed-screen actor placement and era-specific scenery contextualize the scene
Text and inventory or route changes confirm the response
consequences: The social result supports further exploration or restoration
Time travel gains human consequences in addition to terrain effects
observationThemes.factualDescription: Characters and settlements can occupy era-specific states, and interacting with them in the appropriate time can update dialogue, exchange items, and unlock information or progression.
observationThemes.interpretation: World-state mechanics carry more dramatic weight when the same causal comparison applies to people and communities, not only to doors and terrain.

observationThemes.context: The handheld campaign stores persistent temporal capabilities and quest flags while exploration can end in either era of a paired top-down map.
preconditions: The player has acquired a harp tune or other major capability
The session ends or health failure interrupts a route
playerInput: Use available transitions and tools to return to the intended dependency
before: The current era and local route context must be reconstructed
action: Reload the campaign and navigate within the legal paired-map state
after: Persistent items, tunes, essences, and quest flags remain
The needed temporal transition can be performed again
feedback: Status and inventory confirm retained growth
Time-shift effects and stable top-down coordinates show the restored causal state
consequences: A failed hypothesis or encounter can be retried without campaign reset
Temporal dependencies remain testable across portable play sessions
observationThemes.factualDescription: The original single-player save retains tools, harp tunes, essences, and quest state, allowing the player to reconstruct legal past-present routes after resuming or recovering.
observationThemes.interpretation: Stateful puzzles remain fair across interruptions when persistent capability is separated from reconstructible local state and every required transition has a recoverable path.

contributionHypotheses: Paired temporal maps support causal reasoning when stable coordinates anchor a deliberately limited set of meaningful differences.
A world-state mechanic gains narrative force when it changes resident situations and not only traversal geometry.
Multi-room puzzles remain learnable when shared state has a consistent sensory signature and a reconstructible route between cause and effect.
uniqueOrNonportable: The named oracle, region, harp and tunes, eras, essences, characters, exact causal events, dungeon solutions, codes, linked ending, and audiovisual identity are source-specific.
Specific past-present correspondences are evidence only; adaptations must invent independent states, consequences, and geography rather than mirror the source map.
openQuestions: A variant review should verify original link-cable behavior separately from password continuity and later emulation feature loss.
